text:
Digital twin (DT) technology has garnered significant attention across science and engineering due to its potential to revolutionize asset management and operational efficiency. However, the exploration of DT remains fragmented, with manufacturing, aerospace, and oil and gas industries leading the way while civil engineering lags behind. Currently, the civil engineering sector often relies on adapted techniques from these external fields, resulting in a narrow application of DT primarily focused on the operations and maintenance phase. In contrast, Building Information Modeling (BIM) dominates the planning and design stages, yet the highly transient and complex nature of the construction phase continues to hinder seamless DT adoption.

In this article, we propose a comprehensive, phase-based development framework for DT within the architecture, engineering, and construction (AEC) industry. We begin by clarifying the conceptual definitions of DT as both a product and a service, establishing a standardized five-level maturity scale to categorize its capabilities. Furthermore, we conduct a systematic literature review of conventional and emerging techniques utilized at each stage of a civil engineering project—from design through to demolition. In doing so, we identify key enabling technologies, such as computer vision for enhanced environmental sensing and the Internet of Things (IoT) for robust data integration. Ultimately, this work repositions the digital twin as a vital, holistic tool for the entire project life cycle, urging researchers and practitioners to move beyond segmented applications toward an integrated, life-cycle-oriented approach for civil engineering systems.
